reminders: SAY: KS statistic | PSI | WoE | IV | conceptual soundness | benchmarking | back-testing
AML IS risk management — do NOT say "I never did risk"
Self-correction loop — NOT reinforcement learning (RL is Phase 2)
Pick a story → STAR it → STOP. Max 2 minutes.
ASK 2-3 QUESTIONS AT THE END — this is non-negotiable

themes.label: Model validation example
themes.focus: Frame CRS as multi-dimensional validation: conceptual soundness (DWT vs rolling windows), statistical testing (multiple models), scale validation (Pandas → PySpark). This is the strongest direct analog to what RBC does.

themes.label: Statistical testing / KS / PSI / IV
themes.focus: The Validator agent uses KS statistic and Information Value to gate LLM-generated features. If IV falls below threshold, auto-retry. These are the exact metrics RBC uses in credit risk validation.

themes.label: Conceptual soundness / model selection
themes.focus: Diagnosed non-linearity in 7,000 cases BEFORE picking a model — rejected logistic regression on conceptual-soundness grounds. This is literally what validators do before approving a model form.

themes.label: Benchmarking / comparing models
themes.focus: Side-by-side benchmarking of DWT vs rolling windows on sample data. Also validated features across supervised + unsupervised models.

themes.label: Model monitoring / performance drift
themes.focus: Frame Kaggle rank drop as data-drift analog: model underperformed because feature space couldn't represent new patterns. Diagnosed conceptually (not tuning) → rebuilt with RoBERTa hybrid. This is PSI-triggered model refresh logic.

themes.label: Back-testing / out-of-sample performance
themes.focus: Validator agent tests features on held-out validation set using KS + IV. If signal fails, self-correction triggers.

themes.label: Regulatory / traceability / compliance
themes.focus: Every feature traces back to FINTRAC regulatory text. Agent 6 verifies each alert against the regulation. Full audit trail — exactly what OSFI requires.

themes.label: Working with model developers (2LoD dynamics)
themes.focus: Pushed back on a teammate's proposed feature design (rolling windows), explained the conceptual flaw (lost customer-level granularity), proposed alternative (DWT), proved with side-by-side evidence. This is the exact 2LoD-vs-1LoD dynamic RBC cares about.

themes.label: Credit risk / AIRB gap (honest answer)
themes.focus: Be transparent: haven't done AIRB parameters directly. But have done: statistical validation (KS, IV), regulatory modeling (FINTRAC), customer risk scoring, large-scale feature engineering. Methodology transfers; self-studying Basel III / IFRS 9 / PD-LGD-EAD.

themes.label: Risk experience framing
themes.focus: AML is a 2LoD risk function. CRS is customer risk scoring. FINTRAC compliance IS regulatory risk. Don't apologize — reframe.

themes.label: Challenging project
themes.focus: Lead with the bottleneck, then the core challenge: making LLMs produce reliable code with rigorous validation. Emphasize the self-correction loop + Validator agent as the solution.

themes.label: Teamwork / collaboration
themes.focus: Emphasize: 3 people, 3 independent proposals, manager selected yours, you led feature engineering AND validation. Brought teammates along with side-by-side evidence, not authority.

themes.label: Conflict / disagreement / persuasion
themes.focus: Rolling window debate: WHY it breaks customer-level granularity, how you explained DWT in plain language, and the side-by-side evidence that convinced them.

themes.label: Initiative / self-motivation
themes.focus: No one asked you to build this. You read Phume's paper, saw the gap, built the whole system on your own time including the validation layer.

themes.label: Failure / resilience / adaptability
themes.focus: Early success → dropped to 50th → diagnosed as conceptual (not tuning) issue → self-studied RoBERTa → hybrid → back to 2nd. Lesson: initial failure = critical feedback.

themes.label: Receiving feedback / criticism
themes.focus: Frustration first, then mindset shift. Treated critical feedback as roadmap. Re-examined every assumption. Earned endorsement + reference letter.

themes.label: Handling ambiguity / risk mitigation
themes.focus: Two levels: project risk (PoC before full build) and delivery risk (flag delays early). Principle: validate early, communicate early, always have a fallback.

themes.label: Problem-solving / analytical thinking
themes.focus: Diagnostic process: 7,000 case files → non-linearity → rejected LR on conceptual grounds → XGBoost. Right tool, right reason.

themes.label: Large data / scaling
themes.focus: Millions of transactions, 6 FINTRAC channels, Pandas prototype → PySpark production. Know when to scale and why.

themes.label: Prioritization / time management
themes.focus: Three workstreams at Canadian Heritage: dashboards (daily leadership dependency) → forecasting (cross-team) → anomaly detection (internal). Principle: external dependencies first.

themes.label: Explaining complex concepts simply
themes.focus: Cat vs dog analogy for XGBoost over LR. Connects to business world: officers used 20 years of intuition, we needed a model that thinks like them.

themes.label: What do you know about RBC / GRM?

S6_mv_validation.title: S6 → Model Validation Example (MV VERSION)
bullets: [SITUATION] At Scotiabank, our team of three built a Customer Risk Score — a framework that scores customers 0 to 100 based on transactional behavior. Think of it as a behavioral risk scoring model in an AML context, analogous in methodology to a PD-style model.

[TASK — THE VALIDATION CHALLENGE] A core requirement was validating that our engineered features were CONCEPTUALLY SOUND, statistically predictive, and robust across different modeling approaches — not just tied to one model's idiosyncrasies.
[ACTION] I led the validation work on three dimensions.

First — CONCEPTUAL SOUNDNESS. Teammates initially proposed rolling-window features: counts over 30/60/90 day periods. I challenged that design because rolling windows produce multiple rows per customer, which breaks customer-level granularity — you have to collapse the windows, losing information. I proposed Discrete Wavelet Transform as the alternative and BENCHMARKED the two side-by-side on a sample dataset.

Second — STATISTICAL TESTING ACROSS MODELS. We validated features across both supervised and unsupervised models. The principle was: if the signal is real, it should show up regardless of the model we fit. Signal that only appears in one model is suspicious.

Third — SCALE VALIDATION. We prototyped in Pandas, then reran the entire pipeline in PySpark at full scale — millions of rows across 6 FINTRAC channels — and verified results held at production volume.
[RESULT] The feature set passed multi-model validation at production scale.
The CRS framework is now the foundation for Sprint 2 and feeds into the production system.
⭐ KEY TALKING POINTS — 'conceptual soundness', 'benchmarking', 'statistical testing across models', 'scale validation'
⚠️ STOP HERE

S5_mv_testing.title: S5 → Statistical Testing / KS / IV (MV VERSION)
bullets: [SITUATION] At Scotiabank, I built a multi-agent LLM pipeline that auto-generates AML detection features from FINTRAC regulatory text. The core design constraint: NO LLM output could go into production without rigorous validation. LLMs are non-deterministic, so I needed a validation layer that catches everything from code-level bugs to weak predictive signal.
[TASK — THE VALIDATION FRAMEWORK] Design a multi-tier validation system with deterministic pass/fail gates, so every feature that survives is auditable and statistically meaningful.
[ACTION] I built a dedicated Validator agent with three tiers of checks.
First — CODE SAFETY. AST-level analysis to block any generated code that imports dangerous libraries like os or subprocess that could override or delete data.
Second — SCHEMA ALIGNMENT. Verify that every feature only references columns that actually exist in our target data schema. Catches LLM hallucinations immediately.

Third — PREDICTIVE POWER. This is where the STATISTICAL TESTING lives. I use Kolmogorov-Smirnov statistic and Information Value on the validation set. If IV falls below our threshold, the self-correction loop triggers — the Validator feeds structured feedback back to the Engineer agent for automatic retry, up to 5 iterations.
⚠️ SAY 'self-correction loop with structured feedback' — NOT 'reinforcement learning'
Every failed validation attempt is logged for AUDIT TRAIL, and every feature links back to its original regulatory text source. Full traceability.
[RESULT] The pipeline achieved AUC of 90-91.5% on the IBM AML benchmark, with every feature passing the KS + IV gate.
We presented to the SVP and director, and the validation design is one of the reasons leadership is confident enough to explore putting this into production.
⭐ KEY TALKING POINTS — 'KS statistic', 'Information Value', 'audit trail', 'multi-tier validation'
⚠️ STOP HERE

S1_mv_soundness.title: S1 → Conceptual Soundness / Model Selection (MV VERSION)
bullets: [SITUATION] At the Government of Canada, I was tasked with replacing a decades-old manual tax credit review process with a model.

[TASK — CONCEPTUAL SOUNDNESS FIRST] Before building anything, I needed to assess what MODEL FORM was appropriate for the data. Government norms traditionally favored LOGISTIC REGRESSION for interpretability — but I had to check whether the data actually supported that model's underlying assumptions.
[ACTION] I analyzed over 7,000 historical case files to characterize the decision patterns officers had been using for 20 years.

The data showed highly NON-LINEAR, context-dependent relationships — officers weren't following additive linear rules, they were using judgment and interaction effects. This told me logistic regression's linearity and feature-independence assumptions would be VIOLATED on this data. Using LR anyway would have been conceptually unsound.

I chose XGBoost on CONCEPTUAL-SOUNDNESS grounds: tree-based splitting naturally handles non-linearity and feature interactions, and mimics how officers actually make decisions (most discriminating signal first).

Then I presented the diagnostic to policy makers and defended the choice using an analogy — imagine you're telling a cat from a dog: logistic regression forces you to weigh every feature equally, but a tree-based model asks 'does it bark?' first. That's how human intuition works.
[RESULT] 99.7% accuracy, 98% of reviews automated, $700K+ annual savings. Still in production.
⭐ KEY TALKING POINT — 'This is exactly the conceptual-soundness assessment a validator does BEFORE approving a proposed model form.'
⚠️ STOP HERE

S3_mv_monitoring.title: S3 → Performance Monitoring / Model Refresh (MV VERSION)
bullets: [SITUATION] I competed in a Kaggle NLP competition for YouTube spam detection. My initial approach — Logistic Regression + Naive Bayes — got me high on the leaderboard early.

[TASK — THE MONITORING SIGNAL] Over time, my rank dropped below 50th. In banking validation terms, this is a PERFORMANCE MONITORING problem — a signal that the model's performance had degraded as the data distribution shifted. Analogous to what PSI or population stability monitoring would flag in a deployed PD model.
[ACTION] The critical diagnostic question: is this a TUNING issue or a CONCEPTUAL issue?

I diagnosed it as conceptual — the modern spam patterns had semantic complexity that my feature space literally couldn't represent. No amount of hyperparameter tuning on a linear model over bag-of-words features would capture meaning-level patterns.
This is exactly how a validator should think: when a model underperforms, diagnose whether it's data drift, feature-space inadequacy, or model-form mismatch — not just retune.
I self-studied transformer-based models and built a HYBRID: RoBERTa for deep semantic features, TF-IDF for lexical features, fed into an XGBoost + LightGBM ensemble.
[RESULT] Climbed back to 2nd place with over 94% accuracy.
⭐ KEY TALKING POINT — 'The discipline of distinguishing data-drift vs feature-space vs model-form issues is directly what I'd bring to ongoing model monitoring at RBC.'
⚠️ DON'T mention vector dimensions
⚠️ STOP HERE

S5_challenge.title: S5 → Challenging Project
bullets: ⚠️ LEAD WITH THE CHALLENGE STATEMENT — not 3 minutes of background
[SITUATION] At Scotiabank, AML feature engineering is a major bottleneck — translating FINTRAC regulatory documents into executable detection features takes days of manual work by experts.
My senior manager Phume had published a research paper proposing a multi-agent LLM architecture to solve this, but there was no working implementation.
I took the initiative to build the full system from scratch as an independent research project.
[TASK — THE CHALLENGE] The biggest challenge was making LLMs produce production-quality code RELIABLY — getting consistent, validated output from a non-deterministic system.
[ACTION] I designed 6 specialized AI agents, each following a Perceive-Reason-Act loop.

The pipeline reads regulatory text, adapts to the schema, generates Python code, VALIDATES through deterministic + statistical checks (KS + Information Value), runs Isolation Forest detection, and verifies each alert against regulation.

The key solution was the self-correction loop with a dedicated Validator agent: when it catches errors — missing columns, type mismatches, weak predictive signal — it feeds structured feedback back to the Engineer for automatic retry.
⚠️ SAY 'self-correction loop with structured feedback' — NOT 'reinforcement learning'
[RESULT] Regulatory text to executable features in 2-3 minutes versus days manually. AUC 90-91.5% on IBM AML benchmark. Presented to SVP and director.
⚠️ STOP HERE

S5_initiative.title: S5 → Initiative / Self-Motivation
bullets: [SITUATION] At Scotiabank, my senior manager had published a paper proposing a multi-agent LLM architecture for AML feature engineering. There was no working implementation — it was theoretical.
[TASK — THE INITIATIVE] No one asked me to build this. I read the paper, saw the gap, and took it on as an independent project on top of my regular responsibilities.
[ACTION] I designed and built the entire platform: FastAPI backend with 6 specialized agents, each with a distinct role.

The hardest problem was making LLMs produce reliable code. I built a self-correction loop — the Validator catches errors using KS statistic and IV thresholds, then sends structured feedback back for automatic retry.
⚠️ SAY 'self-correction loop with structured feedback' — NOT 'reinforcement learning'
I also built a React frontend for real-time agent monitoring. FastAPI, multi-agent orchestration, and React were all self-taught for this project.
[RESULT] 2-3 minutes vs days manually. AUC 90-91.5%. Director and SVP reviewed. Opening up future research directions for the team.
⚠️ STOP HERE

S5_compliance.title: S5 → Regulatory / Traceability / Compliance
bullets: [SITUATION] At Scotiabank, a core challenge in AML is ensuring every detection feature can be traced back to its regulatory source — FINTRAC. Manual processes preserve this because experts read the regulation directly. Automation has to preserve it too.

[TASK — THE COMPLIANCE CONSTRAINT] I built a multi-agent LLM pipeline to automate feature engineering, with the constraint that every generated feature must maintain FULL TRACEABILITY back to the regulatory text. In a regulated banking environment, you can't have a black box with no audit trail.

[ACTION] I built traceability directly into the architecture. Agent 1 reads FINTRAC text. Agent 2 maps it to the data schema. Agent 3 generates Python. Agent 4 validates deterministically. Agent 5 runs Isolation Forest. Agent 6 verifies every alert against the original regulation — closing the loop.
Every self-correction attempt is logged with structured metadata — what failed, what was fixed — creating an audit trail equivalent to what OSFI-style model audits would require.
[RESULT] AUC 90-91.5% while maintaining full regulatory traceability. Presented to SVP and director.
⭐ KEY TALKING POINT — 'The traceability and audit-logging design I'd apply 1:1 to credit risk model validation documentation.'
⚠️ STOP

S6_teamwork.title: S6 → Teamwork / Collaboration
bullets: [SITUATION] Team of three at Scotiabank, building the Customer Risk Score framework.

[TASK — THE DYNAMIC] All three of us independently proposed feature engineering approaches. My senior manager selected mine — but I still needed buy-in from teammates who had invested in their own proposals.

[ACTION] Rather than overruling, I focused on showing why my approach solved a problem theirs didn't. Rolling windows break customer-level granularity. DWT preserves it. I explained it simply — 'overall trend + short-term frequency in one step' — then showed a side-by-side BENCHMARK on sample data.
Once they were convinced, we worked collaboratively — I led feature engineering and validation methodology, we collectively validated features across supervised + unsupervised models.
Pandas → PySpark at production scale.
[RESULT] Feature set passed multi-model validation. CRS now the foundation for Sprint 2.
⚠️ STOP

S6_conflict.title: S6 → Conflict / Persuasion
bullets: [SITUATION] Team of three building Customer Risk Score. Each person proposed an approach. Manager selected mine — but the others had invested in their own.
[TASK — DISAGREEMENT] My teammates pushed for ROLLING WINDOW features (30/60/90 day counts).

[ACTION] I identified a conceptual flaw: rolling windows produce multiple rows per customer at different time points. Our CRS score has to be ONE number per customer. Merging back loses information or introduces inconsistency.

Rather than 'my way is better,' I explained plainly: 'We need two things — overall trend + short-term frequency. Discrete Wavelet Transform does both in one step and outputs one score per customer. No merging.'
Then I showed a side-by-side BENCHMARK on a sample dataset.
Evidence convinced them. We moved forward together.
⭐ KEY TALKING POINT — 'This is the same dynamic a validator has with model developers: identify the conceptual flaw, explain it clearly, propose the alternative, back it with evidence.'
[RESULT] Feature set passed multi-model validation. CRS now the foundation for Sprint 2.
⚠️ STOP

S6_scaling.title: S6 → Large Data / Scaling
bullets: [SITUATION] Customer Risk Score at Scotiabank. Millions of transactions across 6 FINTRAC channels.
[TASK] Engineer features at customer-level granularity across this volume AND build a pipeline that can process it.
[ACTION] Led feature engineering using DWT — captures trend + frequency in a single pass, no expensive window joins.
Prototyped in Pandas on sample data for fast iteration. Translated to PySpark when cluster access was granted.
Required rethinking Pandas row-indexing vs PySpark's columnar/distributed model, but the core logic transferred because I'd designed features to be aggregation-friendly from the start.
Validated features across supervised + unsupervised models on the full dataset.
[RESULT] Feature set passed validation at full scale. CRS now Sprint 2 foundation.
⚠️ STOP

S1B.title: S1-B → Explaining Complex Concepts Simply
bullets: [SITUATION] Same Canadian Heritage project — after the model was built, I presented to the director and a room of non-technical policy makers.
[TASK] They pushed back: 'Why not stick with logistic regression? That's what we're familiar with.' Had to justify a technical decision to non-technical stakeholders.
[ACTION] I used an analogy: 'Imagine you're telling a cat from a dog.'
'Logistic regression forces you to weigh every feature equally — eyes, nose, tail, sound.'
'But a tree-based model asks one question first: does it bark? That single question gets you 90% of the way.'
'These officers had been doing reviews for 20 years with no written rules, just intuition. We needed a model that thinks like them — focus on the most distinguishing signal first.'
[RESULT] They got it. We moved forward with XGBoost. Still in production today.
⚠️ STOP

S4.title: S4 → Receiving Critical Feedback
bullets: [SITUATION] At Khaveen Investments, a Singapore-based hedge fund, I was preparing my first comprehensive stock pitch.
Received highly critical feedback from my manager — rigorous questioning of assumptions and analytical depth.
[TASK] Immediate reaction was frustration. But the real task was to improve analytical process and learn to handle direct feedback professionally.
[ACTION] Shifted from taking it personally to treating it as a roadmap.
Re-examined every assumption. Hard data backing every conclusion.

(Example): Early on I projected growth based on company's own revenue guidance without cross-referencing analyst consensus. Manager called it out — 'you're taking management at face value.' After that I cross-referenced every projection against ≥2 independent sources before putting it in a pitch.
In subsequent meetings, focused on listening, clarifying questions, responding to substance not pressure.
[RESULT] Analysis quality improved week-over-week. Manager explicitly recognized rapid improvement. Strong endorsement + reference letter for grad school.
⚠️ STOP

S2.title: S2: Orange — EV Data Pipeline
bullets: [SITUATION] At Orange Business Services, our client was a leading global EV manufacturer using our IoT connectivity service.
Massive multi-gigabyte CSV files. Their billing was broken — Excel couldn't handle the volume, preventing accurate invoicing.
[TASK] Solve the data processing bottleneck — automated system for accurate usage-based invoicing.
[ACTION] Moved workflow from desktop to server-side. End-to-end Python + SQL pipeline: auto-ingestion → cleaning → aggregation → usage metrics.
[RESULT] Fixed the revenue cycle. Client placed a follow-up order for 100,000 additional IoT units.
⚠️ STOP

intro_background_mv.title: Walk me through your background (MV framing)
bullets: I'm a data scientist with experience across the full machine learning LIFECYCLE ← pronounce correctly

Currently on the AI and Machine Learning team at Scotiabank, working daily with PySpark, Pandas, and SQL on anomaly detection and customer risk scoring for the AML business line — a 2nd Line of Defense function, so I'm already in a risk-management context.

I also lead a multi-agent LLM pipeline with a built-in validation layer that uses KS statistic and Information Value to gate generated features — the same statistical tests used in credit risk validation.

Before that, and still ongoing, I've been at the Government of Canada — built an XGBoost model that automated 98% of manual reviews, and own end-to-end Power BI pipelines for the Financial Leadership Team.
Honours BSc in Statistics and CS from U of T, finishing my MMA at Rotman this year.
⚠️ EDUCATION LAST — lead with experience

intro_why_rbc.title: Why RBC / Why this role?
bullets: Three reasons.

First, RBC's GRM is one of the most methodologically rigorous model validation groups in Canadian banking — independent 2LoD culture, OSFI-facing, covering everything from AIRB parameters to acquisition and account management models. That's exactly where I want to deepen my expertise.

Second, my Scotiabank work is already in a 2LoD risk context — AML, FINTRAC compliance, customer risk scoring using KS and IV. The methodology transfers directly; the domain shift is from AML risk to credit risk.

Third, the JD explicitly mentions AI/ML techniques and deep learning — RBC is one of the few validation teams actively evaluating how to validate modern ML models alongside traditional PD/LGD/EAD. That's a frontier I want to contribute to.
⚠️ STOP

intro_why_mv.title: Why Model Validation specifically (not building)?
bullets: Honestly — I discovered I liked the validation parts of my projects more than pure building.

In the CRS project at Scotiabank, my favorite part wasn't training models, it was CHALLENGING the rolling-window proposal, benchmarking approaches, and testing features across multiple models to see if signal held.
In my multi-agent pipeline, the most interesting design problem was the Validator layer — what makes an LLM-generated feature trustworthy? KS? IV? Schema checks?

Model validation is where that adversarial, rigorous mindset lives full-time. And in credit risk, it has the highest stakes — these models drive capital requirements and affect real customer outcomes.
⚠️ STOP — don't apologize for not 'wanting to build,' frame it as conscious preference

intro_risk_framing.title: Do you have risk experience? (REFRAME)
bullets: Yes — my Scotiabank role IS in risk management. AML is a core 2nd Line of Defense function.

Specifically, I've worked on: Customer Risk Score — a behavioral risk-scoring framework analogous in methodology to PD modeling. Statistical validation using KS and Information Value. Regulatory modeling with full FINTRAC traceability. Anomaly detection for suspicious transaction identification.
The domain shift from AML risk to credit risk is real, but the methodology — statistical testing, conceptual soundness review, benchmarking, regulatory documentation — transfers directly.
⚠️ Confidence tone — you have risk experience, don't undersell

intro_credit_gap.title: Credit Risk / AIRB Gap (honest bridge)
bullets: I'll be transparent — I haven't validated AIRB parameters directly or worked on Basel-regulated models.

What I HAVE done is the methodological foundation: statistical model validation using KS and IV, regulatory compliance modeling under FINTRAC, customer risk scoring, large-scale feature engineering on transactional data, and model-form conceptual soundness assessment.
I've started self-studying the credit risk domain — PD, LGD, EAD estimation, Basel III capital framework, IFRS 9 ECL — and my MMA at Rotman covers risk finance.
The gap is domain knowledge, not methodology. I'd ramp up on terminology quickly; the underlying statistical discipline is the same.
⚠️ Confidence, not apology

intro_leaving.title: Why leaving Scotiabank?
bullets: My current role has a flexible end date in August. I'm looking for my first full-time role after graduation.
⚠️ STOP — don't mention practicum or return offers

intro_goals_mv.title: Where do you see yourself in a few years?
bullets: Near-term: deepen expertise in credit risk model validation — especially at the intersection of traditional PD/LGD/EAD methods and modern ML techniques. That's where the methodology is evolving most.
Longer-term: take on more technical depth and eventually help shape how the team approaches validating next-generation models — including AI/ML models used in credit decisioning.
RBC's GRM feels like the right environment for that trajectory.
⚠️ STOP

weakness.title: Biggest weakness
bullets: Earlier in my career I tended to over-engineer — reaching for complex models when simpler approaches answered the business question fine.
I've gotten better by forcing myself to start with the simplest viable solution first, and only add complexity when the data justifies it.
Working in government and banking environments helped — non-technical stakeholders force you to prioritize traceability, explainability, and business impact over elegance.
⚠️ STOP

strength_mv.title: Biggest strength (MV framing)
bullets: My strength is combining statistical rigor with end-to-end execution.

On the rigor side — I designed a Validator agent using KS and IV thresholds. I challenged a teammate's feature proposal on conceptual grounds and benchmarked the alternative. I rejected logistic regression at Canadian Heritage on conceptual-soundness grounds before building.
On the execution side — I've shipped end-to-end at every role: SQL extraction to Power BI dashboards at Canadian Heritage, PySpark pipelines to anomaly detection at Scotiabank.
The combination — rigorous analytical thinking plus the ability to actually deliver — is what I'd bring to model validation at RBC.
⚠️ STOP

intro_comp.title: Compensation
bullets: I'm flexible on compensation. The posted range for Senior Analyst roles in GRM aligns with what I'd expect.
⚠️ Don't anchor too specifically until you have the offer

intro_offers.title: Other offers
bullets: I'm actively exploring opportunities and have a few processes in progress. This role is a strong fit for what I'm looking for. Flexible on timeline.
⚠️ STOP

intro_know_rbc.title: What do you know about RBC / GRM?
bullets: RBC is Canada's largest bank by market cap, one of the Big Five.
GRM — Group Risk Management — is RBC's 2nd Line of Defense function, covering credit risk, market risk, operational risk, and model risk across all business lines.

EMRM specifically — Enterprise Model Risk Management — owns independent validation of models that drive regulatory capital (AIRB PD/LGD/EAD), economic capital, and credit decisioning across Wholesale, Retail, and Margin Lending portfolios.

What excites me is that RBC is actively expanding validation methodology to cover AI/ML models — the JD explicitly mentions deep learning and torch — so this team sits at the frontier of where model validation is going.
⚠️ STOP

intro_anything_mv.title: Anything else you'd like me to know?
bullets: Just that I'm genuinely excited about this role.
The combination of rigorous validation methodology, regulated-environment experience, and RBC's scale — it's exactly the depth I want to build my career on.
I'm ready to ramp up on credit risk domain knowledge quickly and contribute on day one with the statistical and validation tooling I already use daily.
⚠️ STOP

prioritization.title: How do you prioritize multiple tasks?
bullets: Framework: external dependencies first, cross-team second, internal third.
At Canadian Heritage I ran three workstreams: Power BI dashboards, revenue forecasting, anomaly detection.
Dashboards first — leadership monitored them every morning, any break directly affected decisions.
Forecasting second — finance and policy teams waited on numbers for budget decisions.
Anomaly detection third — internal project, no external deadline.
If something blocks someone else, it goes to the top regardless.
⚠️ STOP

risk_mitigation.title: Risk mitigation / handling ambiguity
bullets: Two levels.
Project risk — validate early. Before the full multi-agent pipeline, I ran a proof of concept to confirm LLMs could produce usable code at all. Fail fast if the premise breaks.

Delivery risk — communicate early. At Canadian Heritage, when anomaly detection was taking longer because historical data was messier than expected, I flagged it immediately rather than absorb the delay silently. We adjusted together.
Principle: validate early, communicate early, always have a fallback.
⚠️ STOP

my_questions.title: QUESTIONS FOR RBC — ask at least 2
items: What does a typical validation cycle look like — from receiving a model package from the development team to delivering findings?
How does the team balance detailed full validations versus 80/20 approaches? What drives that decision?
What are the most common findings the team escalates back to model developers?
How is the team adapting validation methodology for AI/ML models alongside traditional AIRB parameters?
How closely does the team engage with OSFI audits, and how does that shape the day-to-day work?
What qualities do you see in analysts who thrive in this team?
